md: **Permutations** count *ordered* arrangements. **Combinations** count *unordered* selections. The single most important question in a counting problem: **does order matter?**
## 1. Permutations — Order Matters
A **permutation** is an arrangement of objects where the order matters. The number of ways to choose $r$ items from $n$ distinct items, in order:
Why? First slot: $n$ choices. Second: $n - 1$. Third: $n - 2$. … $r$th: $n - r + 1$. Multiply them.
**Example.** From a class of 20 students, how many ways to elect a president, vice president, and treasurer (3 distinct positions)?
### Special case: arrange ALL of them ($r = n$)
**Example.** How many ways to line up 6 people for a photo? $6! = 720$.
## 2. Combinations — Order Doesn't Matter
A **combination** is a selection where the order doesn't matter. Choosing 'apple, orange' is the same as choosing 'orange, apple.'
Why divide by $r!$? Each combination of $r$ items can be arranged in $r!$ different orders, all of which are 'the same' combination. So we take the permutation count and divide.
**Example.** From a class of 20, how many ways to pick a 3-person committee (no titles)?

blocks.title: Permutation vs combination — the test
blocks.body: **Order matters** (assignments, rankings, sequences, lineups) → **permutation**. **Order doesn't matter** (committee, lottery numbers, hand of cards, group selection) → **combination**. If swapping two of the chosen items gives the 'same' answer, you want a combination.

md: ## 3. The Relationship
Same intuition: $r!$ orderings per combination. Multiply combinations by $r!$ to get permutations.
## 4. Reading Pascal's Triangle as Combinations
The entries of Pascal's Triangle ARE the binomial coefficients $\binom{n}{k}$. Row $n$, position $k$, equals $C(n, k)$.
Try the widget below: click on a cell to see $\binom{n}{k}$ — that's the number of combinations of $k$ items chosen from $n$.
### Worked example — poker
How many 5-card poker hands are there from a 52-card deck?
Order doesn't matter (a hand is a hand). $C(52, 5) = \dfrac{52!}{5! \cdot 47!} = \dfrac{52 \cdot 51 \cdot 50 \cdot 49 \cdot 48}{120} = 2{,}598{,}960$.
About 2.6 million hands.
### Worked example — race
12 runners. Top 3 get gold, silver, bronze.
Order matters (gold ≠ silver). $P(12, 3) = 12 \cdot 11 \cdot 10 = 1320$ ways.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Permutation** $P(n, r) = \dfrac{n!}{(n-r)!}$ — order matters (positions, rankings). **Combination** $C(n, r) = \binom{n}{r} = \dfrac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}$ — order doesn't matter (groups, hands, committees). **First question to ask:** does order matter?
